title: UK Petrol Crosses 150p for First Time in Two Years — What It Actually Costs to Drive to Work Now
description: UK fuel prices have surged past 150p per liter for petrol and 177p for diesel. For the average commuter, that's an extra £500-800 per year. Here's the breakdown.
lede: Filling a family car with unleaded now costs over £82 in the UK — up £9.50 in just one month. Diesel drivers are paying £97 per tank. For millions of commuters with no public transport alternative, these numbers translate to serious annual cost increases.

sections.heading: The Raw Numbers
paragraphs: UK petrol hit 150.2p per liter on Friday — the highest level in nearly two years, according to RAC data. Diesel reached 177p per liter. Unleaded is up 17p since the Middle East conflict escalated. Diesel is up 35p. A standard 55-liter fill-up now costs £82.61 for petrol and £97.35 for diesel.

For context: a year ago, the same fill-up cost about £73 for petrol and £78 for diesel. That's a £9.50 increase for petrol drivers and a staggering £19 increase for diesel in just one month. Over a year of weekly fill-ups, that translates to an additional £500-1,000 in fuel costs.

The RAC called the 150p mark an 'unwelcome milestone' and warned drivers to 'plan carefully' where to refuel, using price comparison apps. The four-day Easter weekend is expected to push demand — and potentially prices — even higher.

sections.heading: The Real Cost of Your Commute
paragraphs: The average UK commuter drives 23 miles each way — 46 miles round trip. In a car averaging 40 MPG (UK gallons), that's 1.15 gallons per trip, or 5.23 liters. At 150p per liter, the daily commute costs £7.84 in fuel alone. Five days a week, 48 weeks a year: £1,882.

A month ago, the same commute cost £6.97 per day (£1,673 annually). The price increase alone adds £209 per year. For diesel drivers doing the same commute at 177p per liter, the annual cost is £2,088 — and the year-on-year increase is even steeper.

These calculations don't include insurance, maintenance, road tax, depreciation, or parking. The AA estimates total car ownership costs at £3,500-5,000 per year for the average UK driver. Fuel is typically 30-40% of that total — and it's the category that fluctuates most violently.

sections.heading: Who's Profiteering?
paragraphs: Asda chairman Allan Leighton rejected claims that retailers are profiteering from the crisis, accusing the government of 'pointing the finger' at forecourts while collecting substantial fuel duty revenue. The current UK fuel duty is 52.95p per liter, plus 20% VAT applied on top of both the fuel cost and the duty — meaning the government takes roughly 70-80p of every liter you buy.

The Competition and Markets Authority has said it will step up monitoring of the fuel sector. The government has warned it will clamp down on any retailers trying to 'rip off' customers. Fuel retailers described the language as 'inflammatory.'

For consumers, the blame game matters less than the bottom line. Whether the cause is geopolitics, profiteering, or taxes, the money leaving your wallet is the same. The only variable you can control is how efficiently you use fuel — which starts with understanding exactly what your commute costs.

sections.heading: What You Can Do
paragraphs: Short-term: use apps like PetrolPrices or Waze to find the cheapest forecourt near you (price differences of 5-10p per liter between nearby stations are common). Drive at steady speeds — 56-65 mph is the sweet spot for most cars. Remove roof racks and heavy items from the boot. Check tire pressures monthly (under-inflated tires increase fuel consumption by 3-5%).

Medium-term: if your commute allows it, consider switching to a hybrid or electric vehicle. The UK government's EV grant still covers up to £2,500 off the purchase price. Charging at home on an economy tariff costs roughly 3-5p per mile versus 15-20p per mile for petrol. The breakeven on an EV vs petrol is typically 3-4 years at current fuel prices.

Use a fuel cost calculator to understand your exact costs — many people significantly underestimate what they spend on fuel. Seeing the annual number (often £1,500-2,500 for regular commuters) can be the catalyst for making changes.

faqs.q: How much does petrol cost in the UK right now?
faqs.a: As of late March 2026, the average UK petrol price is 150p per liter. Diesel is 177p per liter. Filling a standard 55-liter tank costs £82.61 for petrol and £97.35 for diesel.

faqs.q: How much does the average UK commute cost in fuel?
faqs.a: The average UK commuter driving 46 miles round trip in a 40 MPG car spends approximately £7.84 per day in petrol at current prices, or about £1,882 per year (5 days/week, 48 weeks).

faqs.q: Why are UK fuel prices rising in 2026?
faqs.a: The main driver is the US-Israel conflict with Iran disrupting Middle East oil supplies. Crude oil prices have risen significantly, pushing wholesale fuel costs higher. UK fuel duty (52.95p/liter) and VAT add to the consumer price.

faqs.q: How much tax is on UK fuel?
faqs.a: UK fuel duty is 52.95p per liter, plus 20% VAT applied to both the fuel cost and the duty itself. The government takes approximately 70-80p of every liter purchased at the pump.
